you are here
classes and objects
31
50, Brad the 00 guy got the chair, right?
Not 50 fast. Larry found a flaw in Brad’5 approach. And, 
since he was sure that if he got the chair he’d also get Lucy 
in accounting, he had to turn this thing around.
LARRY: You’ve got duplicated code! The rotate 
procedure is in all four Shape things.
BRAD: It’5 a method, not a procedure. And they’re classes, 
not things.
LARRY: Whatever. It’5 a stupid design. You have to 
maintain four different rotate “methods”. How can that 
ever be good?
BRAD: Oh, 1 guess you didn’t see the final design. Let me 
show you how 00 inheritance works, Larry. 
What Larry wanted
(figured the chair would impress her)
They’re Shapes, and they all rotate and 
playSound. 50 1 abstracted out the 
common features and put them into a 
new class called Shape.
Shape
 rotate() 
 playSound() 
Triangle
 
Square
Circle
Amoeba
Shape
 rotate() 
 playSound() 
superclass
subclasses
Then 1 linked the other 
four shape classes to 
the new Shape class, 
in a relationship called 
inheritance. 
Triangle
 rotate() 
 playSound() 
Square
 rotate() 
 playSound() 
Circle
 rotate() 
 playSound() 
1 looked at what all four 
classes have in common.
Amoeba
 rotate() 
 playSound() 
1
2
3
You can read this as, “Square inherits from Shape”, 
“Circle inherits from Shape”, and 50 on. 1 removed 
rotate() and playSound() from the other shapes, 50 now 
there’5 only one copy to maintain.
The Shape class is called the superclass of the other four 
classes. The other four are the subclasses of Shape. The 
subclasses inherit the methods of the superclass. In other 
words, if the Shape class has the functionality, then the 
subclasses automatically get that same functionality.
